mappings.rationale: The primary VSI on evolutionary theory, covering natural selection (B.1.b, C.3), genetic drift (C.2), adaptation and fitness (C.3.a), and speciation (D).
Directly covers Darwin's theory of natural selection (B.1.b), sexual selection (C.3.d), and the concept of species and speciation (D).
Covers the antiquity of life in the geological record (A.4), the fossil record of gradual change (B.2.a), and molecular clues to evolutionary history (B.2.d).
Addresses genetic variation through mutation (C.1), Mendel's theory of heredity (B.1.c), DNA sequencing, and the molecular basis of evolution (B.1.e).
Directly relevant to the fossil record as evidence for evolution (B.2.a) and patterns and rates of species evolution (D.6).
Addresses speciation (D), geochronology for dating the geological record (A.4), and ancient DNA as molecular evidence for evolution (B.1.e).
Directly covers sexual selection (C.3.d) as a type of natural selection, including sexual dimorphism and its role in speciation (D).
Covers Mendel's theory of heredity (B.1.c) and the synthetic theory of evolution (B.1.d) that united genetics with natural selection.
Addresses the genetic code (A.2.d), molecular and genetic studies (B.1.e), and molecular evolution (D.6.c).
Covers the origin of the genetic code (A.2.d) and molecular studies underlying the synthetic theory of evolution (B.1.d and B.1.e).
Covers patterns and rates of species evolution (D.6), including punctuational evolution and mass extinction events in the fossil record.
Addresses vestiges of ancestral forms in embryonic development (B.2.c) as evidence for evolution, including Hox genes and differentiation.
Covers hypotheses about the origin of life (A.1), the primitive atmosphere (A.2.a), and the evolution of photosynthesis and oxygen (A.3.c).
Examines conceptual foundations of evolutionary theory including species concepts (D.1), natural selection (C.3), and essentialism vs. population thinking.
Relevant to the earliest living systems (A.3) including procaryotic cells, and demonstrates evolution through antibiotic resistance (C.3).
Covers the origin of procaryotic and eucaryotic cells (A.3.b), evolution of early life forms, and nitrogen fixation related to early metabolism.
